title: Text-native memory API: remember(text) / recall(query_text)
description: The headline surface that makes nidus a 'memory'. On top of the existing raw upsert/search: remember(collection, id, text, attrs) embeds via the collection's embedder then upserts a Record; recall(collection, query_text, opts) embeds the query (embed_query) then searches. Pin the collection's dimension from embedder.dimension(); stamp embedder_identity() into collection meta so a later recall refuses a mismatched embedder (meaningless cross-space compare). KEEP the raw Vec<f32> upsert/search API untouched for bring-your-own-embedding callers. Wiring the embedder to a Nidus/collection is the main design question — construct-time vs per-collection vs per-call; decide and document. Integrates with existing text_search/FTS (hybrid recall is a possible follow-up).
close_reason: Closed

title: Provider adapters: Voyage, Ollama (dimension probe), OpenAI (index sort)
description: Three Embedder impls behind per-provider features (embed-voyage/embed-ollama/embed-openai, each enabling 'embed'). Voyage: POST {base}/v1/embeddings, input_type document vs query, static dimension-by-model (1024, voyage-3-lite=512), MAX_BATCH 128, requires key. OpenAI: POST {base}/v1/embeddings, sort response by 'index' (order not guaranteed), dimension-by-model (3-large=3072, small/ada=1536), MAX_BATCH 2048, requires key, base_url override serves any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Ollama: POST {host}/api/embed, ASYNC constructor probes dimension (arbitrary user-installed models), keyless by default, sequential batch. Shared post_json helper (auth header, status→Api error, parse). Port over the reverted chirpling prototype (nearly verbatim) incl. its unit + end-to-end mock-socket tests (index reorder, 429→Api error, async dimension probe).
close_reason: Closed

title: Embedder port: trait + EmbedError + EmbedConfig + EmbedProvider
description: The embedding abstraction, feature-gated behind a new off-by-default 'embed' feature. Embedder trait: async fn embed / embed_batch / embed_query (native impl Future+Send, no async_trait/Box) + sync accessors dimension/max_input_tokens/provider_name/model_name. EmbedError (Config/Backend/Api{status,body}/Decode). EmbedConfig plain builder (model, api_key, base_url override, extra_headers — no env/file reading; that's the caller's job). EmbedProvider enum {Voyage,Ollama,OpenAi} with from_name/as_str/default_model/FromStr. embedder_identity(&e) -> 'provider/model'. Embeddings do NOT stream (one call yields the whole fixed-length vector), so the feature pulls plain reqwest (rustls, reusing ring) — NOT reqwest/stream. Lives at the async edge; the sync store core does not depend on it.
close_reason: Closed

title: All-in-one memory: house the AI ingest layer (summarize + embed) in nidus
description:
nidus becomes a true all-in-one 'memory' — text in, relevant text out — owning the single-shot AI transforms that prepare and query what's remembered: SUMMARIZE (optional) + EMBED, then store + retrieve. Callers remember(text[, mode]) and recall(query_text) without pre-computing anything.

Refined principle: a domain is the CAPABILITY, not the vendor. chirpling owns *conversation* (multi-turn, streaming, tool-calling AI participants). nidus owns *memory* (stateless ingest transforms + store + retrieve). Both may hit the same vendors (Anthropic/OpenAI) but are different capabilities with different shapes, so no shared abstraction is forced.

Graph (leaves + composers): chirpling & nidus are independent leaves; wdpkr --> nidus only (feeds text); phx --> chirpling + nidus. wdpkr's summarize/ + ai_providers/ (which already tags providers by Capability{Embed,Summarize}) are the source to port from — DO NOT modify wdpkr as part of this epic.

Accepted trade: nidus's identity widens from 'tight vector store' to 'memory toolkit'. Feature-gated so the DEFAULT build stays a pure, seconds-fast, dependency-lean sync vector store; embed/summarize live behind off-by-default features at the async edge (reqwest reuses existing ring TLS — no new C tree). Embedding half was prototyped in chirpling (reverted) and ports over nearly verbatim.
notes: Rust AI-ingest layer landed on feat/nidus-54l-ai-ingest-memory (v0.28.0): embed (Voyage/OpenAI/Ollama/Cohere/Gemini/Mistral/Jina + openai-compat), summarize (Anthropic/OpenAI-compat), Memory remember/recall (Raw+Summarize), capability registry, feature-gating guard, docs+example. All feature-combo gates green (just ci + ci-ingest). Follow-ups remain open: .11 (JS SDK memory bindings), .12 (docs identity shift).

title: JS SDK: release workflow (publish @duckedup/nidus on version bump)
description: Add .github/workflows/sdk-js-release.yml mirroring release.yml: trigger on push to main with paths sdks/js/**; read version from sdks/js/package.json; release ONLY if tag js-v<version> does not already exist (distinct from the crate's v* tags). Steps: install -> typecheck -> test -> npm publish --access public --provenance with NPM_TOKEN -> GitHub release js-v<version>. Requires the duckedup npm org + NPM_TOKEN repo secret (out-of-band, owner action).

title: JS SDK: unit + integration tests against real nidus serve
description: Unit tests mock fetch and assert exact wire encoding/decoding for value and filter helpers (the bug-prone seam). Integration tests spawn a real 'nidus serve' binary (just build-cli) against a temp dir and run the full lifecycle create -> upsert -> search -> text-search -> hybrid -> stats, mirroring server/mod.rs full_lifecycle_over_http. Add just recipes sdk-js-test / sdk-js-build.

title: JS SDK: scaffold sdks/js + NidusClient + value/filter helpers
description: Scaffold sdks/js as a zero-dependency, cross-runtime (Node 18+/Deno/Bun/browser) TypeScript client published as @duckedup/nidus. Uses the platform-global fetch — no axios/node-fetch. Modules: client.ts (NidusClient — one method per HTTP endpoint: health, stats, collections, createCollection, dropCollection, getMeta, setMeta, upsert, delete, deleteWhere, records, list, search, textSearch, hybridSearch, setFtsSchema, flush, compact), values.ts (v.str/int/bool/list/nil + decode of externally-tagged Value), filter.ts (f.eq/ne/glob/in/notIn/lt/le/gt/ge -> bare predicate array), types.ts (Record/Hit/Footprint/Stats/SearchOpts DTOs), errors.ts (NidusError carrying HTTP status + server {error} message). camelCase opts map to snake_case wire fields; responses decoded to plain JS values. Dual ESM+CJS+.d.ts via tsup. publishConfig access public.
acceptance_criteria: import { NidusClient, v, f } from '@duckedup/nidus' works; every HTTP endpoint has a typed method; value/filter helpers produce exact wire shapes; non-2xx throws NidusError with .status.
close_reason: Scaffolded sdks/js: NidusClient (every HTTP endpoint), v.*/f.* helpers, value encode/decode, typed DTOs, NidusError. Zero-dep, cross-runtime (fetch). Typecheck + build clean.

title: Client SDKs over the HTTP API (sdks/)
description: Provide official client SDKs that drive a running 'nidus serve' over its HTTP API (SPEC §9). Remote-only (not embeddable). Languages: JavaScript/TypeScript first, then Go and Python. Each SDK lives under sdks/<lang>/, versions independently, and ships its own release workflow keyed to a per-language tag namespace (js-v*, go-v*, py-v*) so it never collides with the crate's v* tags. SDKs adapt to the server's wire contract (server/dto.rs), never the reverse — same principle as the nidus binary.

title: Helm chart to deploy nidus serve on Kubernetes
description: A Helm chart (charts/nidus) that runs the duckedup/nidus image on k8s, configured via NIDUS_* values. Single-writer Deployment (replicas=1, Recreate strategy), object-store persistence + Redis memory required (fails fast at render time), bearer-token auth, inline/existing-secret credentials, /health probes, distroless nonroot securityContext. Plus a helm.yml CI workflow (lint + kubeconform render check; publish OCI chart to ghcr.io/duckedup/charts on main) and a Kubernetes docs guide. charts/ + Dockerfile excluded from the crate package.

title: Docker arm64 cross-build fails: missing cross libc headers for ring
description: The publish-docker job failed building the arm64 image: ring's C compile (curve25519.c) errored with 'bits/libc-header-start.h: No such file or directory'. The Dockerfile installed gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu (cross compiler + linker) but not libc6-dev-arm64-cross (cross libc headers), so the cross gcc fell back to the host amd64 /usr/include. Worked locally only because an arm64 host builds arm64 natively. Fix: also install libc6-dev-amd64-cross + libc6-dev-arm64-cross. Verified with a clean --no-cache multi-platform buildx build.

title: Docker workflow never triggers: GITHUB_TOKEN tag push can't start it
description: docker.yml listened on push:tags, but release.yml pushes the v<version> tag with the default GITHUB_TOKEN, which by GitHub's anti-recursion rule cannot trigger another workflow. So the Docker image is never built on release (confirmed: v0.25.0 released with no Docker run). Fix: make docker.yml a reusable (workflow_call) + dispatchable workflow taking an explicit version input, and call it from release.yml's publish-docker job gated on should_release.

title: Dockerfile + env-var config for nidus serve (object-store + Redis only)
description: Publish the nidus HTTP server as a Docker image driven entirely by environment variables, as the precursor to a Helm chart. Add native clap env support (NIDUS_*) to the serve args, a --require-remote flag that restricts the container to non-local backends (s3://|gs://|gcs:// persistence + redis-family memory), a distroless multi-stage Dockerfile, a .dockerignore, and a GitHub Actions workflow that builds and pushes multi-arch images to Docker Hub on release tags.

title: CAS-fence the stale writer-lock reclaim (TOCTOU)
description: try_claim's stale-lock reclaim did an unconditional delete-then-create, which could delete a lease a live holder renewed in the get->delete gap (stealing a live lease). Fixed: capture the stale object's CAS token via get_cas and reclaim with a conditional put_cas(expected=token); a holder that renewed (token moved) or a peer that reclaimed first now defeats the reclaim cleanly. Falls back to delete+create only on backends without conditional-overwrite CAS.

title: BM25 full-text + hybrid search (epic)
description: Add opt-in BM25 full-text search alongside vector search, plus hybrid (RRF) fusion. Per-collection FTS field schema (declarable at create or later), hand-rolled English analyzer (lowercase+segment+stopwords+Porter stem), on-disk fts cache, optional vectors (text-only docs). Shared SecondaryIndex trait + index_cache codec to dedupe ANN/FTS. Plan: /Users/austin/.claude/plans/piped-sparking-wozniak.md
close_reason: All children complete: shared index_cache codec, optional vectors, FTS core (analyzer+BM25), store wiring, text_search, hybrid (RRF), fts cache, CLI+server, docs+0.14.0. Shipped in PR #25.

title: Server: full network parity (GET /stats) + dedicated HTTP API docs page
description: Server lacked a /stats endpoint (footprint+dimension+distance) that the CLI has. Add it for full library/CLI parity over HTTP. Reframe docs: dedicated HTTP server & API reference page with a complete network-only (curl) workflow; slim the server section in cli-and-server; add sidebar entry; update README.
close_reason: Closed

title: nidus backup/restore CLI commands (.tar.gz snapshots)
description: Add 'nidus backup' (snapshot store dir to a single pure-Rust .tar.gz) and 'nidus restore' (extract back, confirm-before-overwrite with -y). Hot backup: capture data-then-log for a consistent lock-free snapshot. Pure-Rust deps only (tar, flate2 rust_backend), cli-feature gated. Bump 0.9.0 -> 0.10.0, sync docs/README.
close_reason: Implemented nidus backup/restore (.tar.gz, pure-Rust tar+flate2 rust_backend), version 0.10.0, docs+README synced; just ci-cli green

title: server: all HTTP errors return 500, including bad input
description: ApiError maps everything to 500. Map dimension mismatch->400, read-only->403, capacity->507, lock->409.
close_reason: Fixed in this branch: docs multi-metric/quant/filters, HTTP body limit (256MiB default, configurable), 4xx/507 status mapping, optional bearer auth + 0.0.0.0 warning, RwLock for concurrent reads, install.sh sha256 verification, friendlier lock error.

title: server: HTTP upsert capped at axum's 2MB default body limit
description: axum 0.8 Json extractor enforces a 2MB body limit; real embedding batches over HTTP get 413. Make it a large configurable limit.
close_reason: Fixed in this branch: docs multi-metric/quant/filters, HTTP body limit (256MiB default, configurable), 4xx/507 status mapping, optional bearer auth + 0.0.0.0 warning, RwLock for concurrent reads, install.sh sha256 verification, friendlier lock error.

title: Make quantization upsert incremental (fix O(N)-per-batch rebuild)
description: upsert currently calls rebuild_quant() which rescans all vectors for min/max and re-quantizes the whole matrix on every batch -- O(N) per batch, quadratic for incremental indexing (a stated use case). Fix: quantize only newly-appended rows against existing params; refit params geometrically (when row count outgrows the fit set by 2x) for amortized O(1) per row.
close_reason: Quantization fixed (symmetric int8), benchmarked, made incremental; PR A

title: Benchmark int8 quantization: recall vs rescore, speed vs exact
description: Add a dedicated quantization benchmark to nidus-bench that sweeps the rescore factor (1/2/4/8), measuring recall@k against independent exact ground truth and query speedup vs the f32 brute-force path. Goal: prove quantization wins and pick the default rescore from data.
close_reason: Quantization fixed (symmetric int8), benchmarked, made incremental; PR A

title: Metadata-only queries (search without a vector)
description: Allow search to accept no query vector, returning filter-matched records without scoring. Fills the gap where callers want 'give me everything where lang=rust' without fabricating a dummy vector.
close_reason: Closed

title: int8 scalar quantization
description: Store int8 quantized vectors alongside f32. Scan quantized copies for 4x less memory and faster dot, then f32-rerank top candidates. Extends exact search regime before needing ANN. Already specced as deferred seam in SPEC §9.
close_reason: Shipped: model::Quantization + store::QuantState + two-pass search_quantized (int8 first pass → f32 rerank), since extended with incremental upsert (0.6.1) and parallel int8 first pass (0.7.1). Stale claim.

title: Add distance metric selection (Cosine, Euclidean, DotProduct)
description: Currently nidus only supports cosine similarity. Add a Distance enum (Cosine, Euclidean, DotProduct) to Config, pinned at store creation in the data header (byte 12, backward-compatible). Cosine normalizes on insert; Euclidean and DotProduct store raw vectors. All scores follow 'higher = more relevant'.
close_reason: Shipped: model::Distance { Cosine, Euclidean, DotProduct } pinned in the data header, selected via Config::distance. Stale in-progress claim never closed.

title: Atomic appends + batch rollback (data & log) — prevent ENOSPC corruption
description:
DataSegment::append and OpLog::append each do a single write_all; a partial write on ENOSPC leaves a partial row / torn frame, and the next append writes past it → misaligned matrix or a mid-file torn frame that parse_all_frames rejects as hard Corruption on reopen. A process that catches the Err and keeps writing corrupts the store.

Fix:
- src/data/mod.rs::append: capture pre-write offset (stream_position); on write_all error, set_len+seek back so a torn partial row never persists (atomic per row). New truncate_to(rows) batch-rollback primitive (file set_len to HEADER+rows*stride, seek, truncate vectors to rows*dim; works file+in-memory).
- src/log/mod.rs::append: same atomic treatment (roll back partial frame on failure). New offset()->u64 and truncate_to(offset) primitives.
- src/store/mod.rs::upsert becomes a transaction: capture data_mark=row_count & log_mark=offset; reserve up-front; append vectors (rollback data on err); sync data (rollback on err); append log ops (rollback log+data on err); sync log (rollback both); THEN commit to in-RAM index (infallible, capacity pre-reserved). Other log writers (delete/drop/set_meta/create_collection) rely on per-frame atomic append.

Tests: truncate_to round-trips (file-backed, miri-ignored); log offset/truncate_to drop trailing frames and replay remainder; end-to-end batch rollback via a #[cfg(test)] fail_after seam on DataSegment forcing a mid-batch append error, asserting row_count/file len/log offset/index all restored, and reopen consistent.
acceptance_criteria: A failed append (ENOSPC mid-batch) leaves the store byte-identical to its pre-call state; reopen replays cleanly with no Corruption error; new truncate_to/offset primitives covered by file-backed tests; just ci + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented + verified: atomic appends & all-or-nothing upsert (data+log rollback), try_reserve OOM→Err across vector matrix + index maps with streaming open, max_vector_bytes cap + footprint(), and per-module SPEC.md consolidated into root SPEC.md. just ci + just miri green; cargo doc clean.

title: Graceful failure under resource exhaustion
description: nidus keeps every vector in RAM and on disk. Today, running out of room fails badly: (1) a partial write_all on ENOSPC corrupts the data matrix / op-log mid-file; (2) Vec/HashMap growth aborts the process on OOM instead of returning Err; (3) no footprint introspection or capacity cap. This epic turns exhaustion aborts into recoverable errors, makes batch writes all-or-nothing so a failure can never corrupt the store, and adds a deterministic vector-bytes cap + footprint() so hosts can refuse work proactively (the only defense reliable under cgroup/overcommit SIGKILL). Stays in-RAM brute-force; NOT spill-to-disk/mmap/ANN. Pure safe Rust, forbid(unsafe_code) intact (try_reserve is safe std).
close_reason: All child issues complete; graceful-failure epic delivered.

title: JS SDK: remember()/recall() memory bindings over HTTP
description: Extend the @duckedup/nidus JS SDK (nidus-70x) with the text-native memory surface once nidus serve exposes it: remember(collection, id, text, {mode}) and recall(collection, queryText, opts) over the HTTP API. Server-side embedding/summarization means the SDK sends text, not vectors. Mirrors the Rust memory API (nidus-54l.4/.10). Depends on the HTTP server growing memory endpoints. Also fold provider/model selection into the client options.

title: remember(text, Summarize) — summarize-then-embed ingest mode
description: Wire the summarizer into the memory ingest path. remember(collection, id, text, mode) gains a Summarize mode: run the collection's summarizer, then embed the summary, then store — vs Raw mode (embed the text directly, the docstring-style path). Store both the summary and a pointer to source text in attrs so recall hits are explainable. Mode is per-call (or a collection default). recall stays embed-query→search. This is the headline 'all-in-one' behavior: raw source in via wdpkr, relevant text out.
close_reason: Closed

title: Summarizer adapters: Anthropic (+ OpenAI-compatible), single-shot
description: Concrete Summarizer impls behind per-provider features (summarize-anthropic, summarize-openai). Anthropic: POST {base}/v1/messages non-streaming, system+user prompt, requires key, base_url override for gateways/Bedrock shim. OpenAI-compatible: POST {base}/v1/chat/completions non-streaming (serves Azure/LiteLLM/vLLM/Ollama /v1 via base_url). Shared single-shot request/parse helper (mirrors the embed post_json). Port from wdpkr src/ai_providers/anthropic.rs (summarizer). Unit + mock-socket tests for request shape and response parse.

description: Sibling to the Embedder port (nidus-54l.1), behind a new off-by-default 'summarize' feature. Summarizer trait: single-shot async fn summarize(text, opts) -> String (NON-streaming — get the whole summary back; nidus does not want chirpling's streaming/tool Responder machinery). SummarizeError (Config/Backend/Api/Decode), SummarizeConfig plain builder (model, api_key, base_url override, extra_headers, max_tokens, + prompt/system knobs). Prompt templates ported from wdpkr's summarize/prompts.rs. Reuses the same reqwest (rustls/ring) already pulled by the embed feature — no new C. Async edge; sync store core does not depend on it. Source to port from: wdpkr src/summarize/ (DO NOT modify wdpkr).

description: Prove the AI ingest layer (embed + summarize) does not regress nidus's identity. 'embed', 'summarize', and the per-provider features all OFF by default. CI: assert the DEFAULT build (none of them) still 'builds in seconds' with the sync core unchanged and no new deps; a separate lane builds the ingest features. description: Closed enum over the shipped providers for runtime selection from a config string: AnyEmbedder::build(EmbedProvider, EmbedConfig) -> Result<Self> (async — Ollama probes on construction), impl Embedder delegating per variant. Add an opt-in Rust HTTP client (e.g. nidus::client behind a feature) that reuses server::dto wire types so the contract has one source of truth, giving Rust the same remote reach as the JS/Go/Python SDKs. Surfaced while planning the JS SDK.

title: Support Redis/Valkey Cluster for the memory tier
description: The RedisTier used a single-node redis::Client; a redis:// URL at a cluster node fails on MOVED/ASK redirects. Added ?cluster=true to open a redis::cluster::ClusterClient (seed-node discovery, slot routing). Single-node and cluster connections share one path via Box<dyn ConnectionLike> + low-level Cmd. New deps: crc16+rand (tiny). Docs + chart values updated.
close_reason: Implemented in this change: ?cluster=true → ClusterClient with slot routing + MOVED/ASK handling.

title: Support keyless cloud auth: S3 IRSA/instance-role + GCS Workload Identity
description: Today the S3 backend requires static AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/SECRET (src/backend/s3.rs:66, bails otherwise) and GCS requires a service-account key JSON (src/backend/gcs.rs:57). Neither does the cloud-native keyless token exchange: S3 can't use EKS IRSA (AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE + AWS_ROLE_ARN -> STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) or the EC2/ECS instance metadata role; GCS can't use GKE Workload Identity / the metadata server token. Add these so pods can auth without long-lived keys. Surfaced while documenting the Helm chart (the chart now correctly states static-creds-only). Sans-IO constraint: the STS/metadata calls would go through the existing ureq Http client; watch the dep/build-time budget.
close_reason: Implemented: S3 keyless auth (IRSA web-identity/STS, ECS task role, EC2 IMDSv2) + GCS metadata-server (Workload Identity), with refresh. New src/backend/aws_creds.rs.

title: Reject quantization + per-segment indexing (silent no-op)
description: When Config::quantization and per-segment indexing (segment_max_rows + segment_index_min_rows) are both set, once a segment seals and is IVF-indexed search dispatches to search_segmented, which never consults the quantized matrix — so quantization was built and maintained at full memory/CPU cost yet had no effect (a silent semantic surprise). Fixed: Config::validate() rejects the three-way combination at open with a clear error. Sealing without indexing (segment_max_rows alone) keeps quantization fully in effect and stays allowed. A future option is to make the segmented legs honour quantization (per-segment IVF in quant space + quantized tail).

title: Phase 2: per-segment IVF index at compaction + exhaustive tail
description: Build an IVF (centroid/list) index once per immutable segment at merge/compaction time; score small segments and the unindexed WAL tail exhaustively (exact). Makes 'exact vs approximate' a per-segment, size-driven property rather than a global mode — laptop stores stay 100% recall/zero-config. Reuse ivf.rs; IVF over HNSW for the object-backed path (lower roundtrips/write-amplification). SPEC §14.3.
close_reason: Phase 2 shipped: per-segment IVF over immutable cold segments + exhaustive active/small-segment tail, opt-in via Config::segment_index_min_rows (default off → fully exact). Fan-out search merges brute-force tail + per-segment IVF walks into one exact-reranked top-k; shared offer_candidates dedupes the candidate-resolution logic with the global ANN path. Reuses ivf.rs. just ci (420) + ci-cli (442) + miri + docs all green. Deferred follow-ups (cache persistence, parallel/quantized per-segment walks, tuning, background merge) filed separately.

title: Phase 1: segment format + manifest + WAL→segment replay
description: Replace the monolithic data matrix + single log with: immutable SEGMENT objects (vectors + attrs + optional index slot), a write-ahead log for in-flight writes, and a tiny MANIFEST naming the live segments (the atomic commit point). On open, replay the WAL into segments and read through the manifest. Generalize the §6.2 lock-free reader rule from 'ignore rows past size S' to 'read the manifest, read the segments it names'. Additive over the append-only format; no §4 API change. SPEC §14.2/§14.6 phase 1.
close_reason: Phase 1 shipped: segment format + manifest + WAL→segment seal + transparent legacy migration; brute-force/quant/ANN unchanged via dense global row space; just ci + miri + ci-cli all green

title: Segment-based storage: the scale model (SPEC §14)
description:
Rearchitect the storage layer so scaling is a quantity of one architecture, not a separate mode: durable objects on the persistence backend are the source of truth, a running process is a cache over them, and the single in-RAM data matrix + one log become immutable SEGMENTS + a WAL + a manifest. Fully designed in SPEC §14 (PR #32). Brute-force stays the exact default for the small/recent tail; IVF indexes the cold bulk (per-segment, size-driven). No public API change (§4).

## Context
Grew out of the cluster-mode discussion: cluster mode should be a CONSEQUENCE of this model (shared backend + versioned manifest + race-free writer lease from nidus-a7c), requiring shared persistence + shared memory tier; local FS/RAM are single-node by definition.
design: See SPEC §14. Principle (§14.1): object store is truth, compute caches it. Segments (§14.2) are the unit of >RAM / incremental cloud writes / distribution / maintainable per-segment indexing / background-merge compaction. Brute-force is the tail not the engine (§14.3); IVF over HNSW for the object-backed path. Log-first async indexing (§14.4). Non-negotiables (§14.5): exact/zero-config local, forbid(unsafe), <1min build, §4 API unchanged. Phasing front-loads single-node payoff (§14.6).
close_reason: All 5 phases shipped (0.18.0–0.22.0): segment format + manifest + WAL→segment seal (vuq.1), per-segment IVF + exhaustive tail (vuq.2), per-segment mmap (vuq.3), manifest-versioned reader refresh (vuq.4), cooperating-instances cluster mode (vuq.5). The monolith is gone: a store is immutable segments + a WAL + a versioned manifest; scale is a quantity of one architecture (local FS → object store → cooperating instances) via config, not a separate mode. Exact-by-default and the §4 public API held throughout. Follow-ups tracked in nidus-bdg (refresh) and nidus-ahw (cluster hardening).

title: Phase 3b: Live S3/GCS backing store (ObjectAppender whole-object rewrite + persistence-as-config-choice + advisory object lock)
description: Make S3/GCS a LIVE persistence choice, not just snapshot. title: DECISION: zero-FFI vs C-TLS for cloud storage backends (SPEC §13.6)
description: Cloud backends (S3/GCS) need HTTPS → a TLS stack. Verified dep trees: rustls defaults to aws-lc-sys (C); ring provider compiles C+asm; reqwest's rustls path pulls ring; google-cloud-storage pulls ring AND OpenSSL. A pure path exists (rustls + rustls-rustcrypto + hand-built hyper/tokio-rustls = no C) but rustls-rustcrypto is UNAUDITED. S3 auth (Sigv4) is pure HMAC (rusty-s3 clean); GCS auth (tame-oauth) pulls ring for RSA-JWT and would need RustCrypto rsa instead.
acceptance_criteria: Pick (A) Pragmatic: allow audited C TLS (aws-lc/ring) gated behind a non-default feature like cli, so cargo add nidus stays pure — OR (B) Purist: hold zero-FFI via rustls-rustcrypto + hand-rolled auth (bespoke, unaudited crypto). Redis over plain TCP (redis://) needs no TLS and is unaffected; only rediss:// reuses this. Blocks the S3/GCS phase.
notes:
RESOLVED → (A) pragmatic, NOT purist. C/FFI allowed but ONLY behind a non-default 'cloud' feature AND only via crates that don't compile a large C tree. Hard rule: cloud-feature clean build < 1 minute. FORBIDDEN (slow C compile): rustls default aws-lc-sys, vendored OpenSSL, reqwest+tokio+hyper. ALLOWED (fast): sans-IO rusty-s3/tame-gcs + lightweight blocking HTTP (ureq) + TLS via ring or system-linked native-tls. MEASURED: clean debug build of rusty-s3+tame-gcs+ureq (ring included) = ~6s, ~10x under budget. (B) purist rejected: unaudited crypto on a credentialed path is a worse trade than a fast, feature-gated, audited C dep. See SPEC §13.6.
SUPERSEDED detail: NOT feature-gated. All backends compile into the DEFAULT build (no per-backend / no 'cloud' feature). Rationale: a user who outgrows local FS and needs S3 must switch with a one-line runtime change (file:// -> s3://), never a Cargo.toml-edit-and-recompile event. Accepted cost: ring enters the default tree (C toolchain required for all consumers; Miri now covers only our logic, network TLS tests join the miri-ignore set). Measured: full core + all four backends (sync Redis, no tokio/aws-lc/OpenSSL) clean build = ~7.5s. See SPEC §1 + §13.6.
close_reason: Closed

title: Pluggable storage & memory backends: persistence (local/S3/GCS) + shared memory tier (local RAM/Redis/Valkey/Memcached)
description: Generalize the SPEC §5 local directory into a pluggable storage layer: one object-granular, sync `Backend` trait with impls for local files, S3, GCS, and Redis/Valkey, selected by URL scheme (file://, s3://, gs://, redis://). Fully designed in SPEC §13. The on-disk object set (data/log source-of-truth + ann/fts derived caches) is unchanged — only where the bytes live. Search stays backend-independent (never in the query hot path); network backends trade cheaper near-incremental writes for a costlier open (rebuild caches from the download). Both live-backing and snapshot/backup modes are in scope.
design: See SPEC.md §13. Two object classes: source-of-truth (data, log; append-shaped, must be durable) vs derived cache (ann, fts; reconstructable, droppable). Trait is sync by design (§13.2): search is CPU-over-RAM, every backend can be sync (sans-IO S3/GCS + blocking HTTP; redis-rs blocking mode), and a sync trait is dyn-safe for true plug-and-play. Async-only clients quarantine block_on internally.
notes: RESHAPE: this is TWO orthogonal axes, not one storage trait. (1) PERSISTENCE backend = durable source-of-truth bytes (data/log): local files / S3 / GCS. (2) MEMORY TIER = where the in-RAM working set is held for serving: local process RAM (default) / Redis / Valkey / Memcached. Redis/Valkey/Memcached are NOT storage — they are a SHARED, rebuildable cache of the working set (model (a)): loaded into local RAM to serve, never searched over the wire, never authoritative, never larger-than-RAM. The two axes compose. See SPEC §13 (rewritten).

design:
# ANN / HNSW approximate index — opt-in, brute-force stays the default

## Summary
A pure-Rust approximate-nearest-neighbour index (HNSW, or IVF) built over the same
`data` rows. When enabled, `search()` consults the index instead of the full scan.
**Exact brute-force remains the default and is never silently replaced** — ANN is opt-in
via `Config` only. Needed only past brute-force's comfort zone (≫ a few million vectors).

## Positioning decision (not just engineering)
This shifts nidus from "exact" to "**exact by default, approximate opt-in**." That is a
deliberate identity change — keep the default exact, make the opt-in explicit and named,
and document the recall/speed trade honestly. Do not let ANN leak into the default path.

## Constraints (non-negotiable, same as the rest of nidus)
- Pure Rust, **no `*-sys`, no FFI**; `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` in our code; Miri-clean.
- Additive over the existing on-disk format — the index is an in-RAM structure
  rebuildable from `data` (persistence is a later sub-seam, not v1).

## Config surface (sketch)
- `Config::index(IndexKind::Hnsw { m, ef_construction, .. })`, default `IndexKind::None`
  (brute force). Per-query `ef`/recall knob in `SearchOpts`.

## Design notes (high-level — this is a future epic, own branch)
- Incremental graph build on `upsert`; handle deletes/overwrites (tombstones + periodic
  rebuild on `compact`).
- Composes with quantization: HNSW over int8/binary codes is a common, effective combo
  (cheap distance in the graph walk, f32 rerank at the end).
- Benchmark the brute-force↔ANN crossover point to document *when* to enable it.

## Sequencing
After the binary-quant + scan-cache work lands (this is the follow-on epic). Large enough
to warrant its own branch and incremental PRs.

title: Binary quantization first-pass (sign-bit codes, Hamming/popcount, f32 rerank) + shared scan-order cache
description: Add a binary (1-bit/component) first-pass tier to the existing two-pass quantized search: 32x smaller first-pass matrix than f32 (8x smaller than int8), Hamming kernel via u64 popcount (pure Rust, zero FFI, Miri-clean). Cosine-only (sign codes are an angular proxy). Opt-in via Config::quantization; brute-force f32 stays the default. Folds in nidus-dxt (cache the row-sorted scan order) so all scan paths share one row-sorted index. No on-disk format change; no new deps.
design:
# Binary quantization first-pass + shared scan-order cache

## Motivation
The f32 first pass is **bandwidth-bound** — the documented ceiling on parallel-scan
gains. int8 cut first-pass memory traffic 4×; binary cuts it **32×** (8× smaller than
int8), so the first pass scales far better with `query_threads`. The kernel is
`u64::count_ones()` (POPCNT) — a single instruction, autovectorizes, **pure safe Rust**,
so it stays **Miri-clean and zero-FFI**. No new dependencies; `just deps` stays empty.

## Soundness / scope — COSINE ONLY
Sign-bit codes approximate **angular (cosine)** similarity (SimHash): for unit vectors,
the Hamming distance between sign vectors is monotone with the angle. It **discards
magnitude**, so it is NOT a sound ranking proxy for `DotProduct` (magnitude matters) or
`Euclidean` (L2). Therefore binary quantization is supported for `Distance::Cosine`
**only** — reject at `open()` with a clear error for the other metrics (suggest int8).
The f32 rerank still yields exact cosine scores for the selected candidate set; binary
only governs candidate *selection*, exactly like the int8 first pass.

## Config surface
Generalize `Quantization` to carry the scheme:
```rust
pub enum QuantKind { Int8, Binary }
pub struct Quantization { pub kind: QuantKind, pub rescore: usize }
impl Quantization {
    pub fn int8()   -> Self { Self { kind: QuantKind::Int8,   rescore: 4  } } // == current Default
    pub fn binary() -> Self { Self { kind: QuantKind::Binary, rescore: 16 } } // coarser → higher overscan
    pub fn rescore(mut self, n: usize) -> Self { self.rescore = n; self }
}
impl Default for Quantization { fn default() -> Self { Self::int8() } }
```
- Small breaking change to the struct literal; acceptable pre-1.0 (0.10.x). Update call
  sites + docs (`config.rs` doc, getting-started, README if shown).
- `Config::quantization: Option<Quantization>` is unchanged in shape; **brute-force f32
  remains the default** (`None`).
- Binary's default `rescore` is higher (16) because the proxy is coarser; final value is
  a tuning knob to settle by benchmark.

## Storage (in-RAM only — NO on-disk format change)
- `BinState { words: Vec<u64>, words_per_row: usize }`, `words_per_row = dim.div_ceil(64)`.
  Memory = `N * words_per_row * 8` ≈ `N*dim/8` bytes.
- Derived from the f32 `data` rows: bit `i` set iff component `i >= 0.0` (treat ±0.0 as
  `>= 0`). Tail bits past `dim` in the last word are zero in both stored rows and query,
  so they never affect the XOR.
- **Scale-free**: unlike int8 there is no `QuantParams`/global scale and no
  `REFIT_GROWTH`. A row's code depends only on its own signs → incremental `upsert` is
  trivially O(batch), append-only, and never needs a refit.
- Refactor the store's quant slot to hold either scheme:
  `enum Quant { Int8(Int8State), Binary(BinState) }`, `quant: Option<Quant>`.

## Kernels (`search/mod.rs`)
- `fn hamming(a: &[u64], b: &[u64]) -> u32` — XOR + `count_ones`, chunked/lane-folded for
  ILP like `dot()`; pure integer ⇒ Miri-clean.
- First-pass score = `-(hamming as f32)` so higher = better (fits the existing `TopK`
  max-semantics) and is monotone with cosine rank for unit vectors.
- `pack_query(q: &[f32]) -> Vec<u64>` using the same sign rule on the normalized query.

## Two-pass wiring (`store/mod.rs`)
- Mirror `search_quantized`: a `score_chunk_bin` analogous to `score_chunk_i8`, fed
  through the existing generic `parallel_topk` (already generic over `T`/`score_one`)
  when `workers > 1`, else serial. Overscan `top_k * rescore` candidates carrying
  `(row, col, id)`.
- f32 rerank of candidates with the real `score_fn`; `min_score` enforced on the f32
  score only (Hamming is an ordering proxy) — identical contract to int8.

## Scan-order cache — folds in nidus-dxt (option 3)
`search()`/`list()` rebuild `scan: Vec<(row, col, id)>` and `sort_unstable_by_key(row)`
on EVERY query (~29% of a 100k/dim768 serial query). The doc set only changes on write.
- Maintain a **cached row-sorted scan index**, updated on the `&mut self` write paths
  (`upsert`/`delete`/`delete_where`/`drop_collection`/`compact`) and read by the `&self`
  search/list paths with no per-query sort.
- **Key insight:** `data.append` returns monotonically increasing rows, so a brand-new
  id always appends to the END of the row order → O(1) maintenance for the common
  indexing path. Overwrites (old row → dead) and deletes remove an entry; flag those and
  do a lazy full rebuild on next read (dxt's recommended low-risk option (a)).
- **Sync constraint:** store runs under `Arc<RwLock>`/`Mutex`, so no `RefCell`. Prefer
  rebuild-eager-on-write (cache is a plain field; appends keep it sorted incrementally;
  deletes/overwrites set a dirty flag → rebuilt on next read) so `search()` stays
  lock-free. Falls back cleanly to current behavior under write-heavy churn.
- Composes with the parallel path: workers take pre-sorted contiguous slices and skip the
  per-chunk sort entirely (dxt noted this).
- MUST stay correct under multi-collection scope, filters (filter after taking sorted
  order — order preserved), dead rows (append-only overwrite), and Miri-clean.

## Testing
- Pure-logic (Miri-clean): `hamming` vs naive across lengths incl. tail bits; sign-pack
  round trip; binary first-pass recall vs exact on synthetic clustered data
  (`recall@k ≥ threshold` for a given `rescore`).
- Reject `Binary` + non-Cosine at `open()`.
- Scan cache: equivalence — same query yields identical ranked ids/scores with cache
  on vs off; correctness under overwrite/delete/multi-collection/filter.
- Bench (`just bench-crit`): new group comparing binary vs int8 vs f32 (latency +
  recall) at dim 384/768, n=100k/1M; sweep `rescore`. Compare scan-cache against the
  `nidus-33k-final` baseline — only keep the cache if it beats current numbers without
  complicating the write path.

## Done =
- Opt-in; default behavior unchanged (brute-force f32).
- `just ci-cli` green, `just miri` green, `just deps` still empty (no new crates).
- Move binary quant in `SPEC.md` §9 from "still deferred" → "shipped"; update the
  CLAUDE.md deferred-seam list; bump docs/README version snippet if a release goes out.
acceptance_criteria: Opt-in, default behavior unchanged (f32 brute force). just ci-cli + just miri green. just deps still empty. Binary first-pass recall@k meets threshold at the chosen rescore; bench shows it beats int8 on latency for cosine. Binary+non-cosine rejected at open. Scan-cache: identical ranked results cache on/off, correct under overwrite/delete/multi-collection/filter. On ship: move binary quant SPEC §9 deferred->shipped + update CLAUDE.md.
notes:
2026-06-09 PROGRESS — binary quantization COMPLETE on austin/spec-9:
- model.rs: QuantKind{Int8,Binary} enum; Quantization::int8()/binary()/rescore(); Default=int8 (behavior unchanged). QuantKind exported from lib.
- search/mod.rs: pack_signs/pack_signs_into + hamming (u64 popcount, lane-folded, pure/Miri-clean) + 11 kernel tests.
- store/mod.rs: Int8State/BinState/Quant enum (replaces QuantState); scale-free binary (no params/refit); pack_matrix; rebuild_quant/extend_quant variant-aware; Quant::empty() gates binary+non-cosine at open(); two-pass search_int8/search_binary + shared rerank_candidates; score_chunk_bin via the generic parallel_topk.
- Tests: cosine-only gate, ranking, multiword state coverage, recall@10>=0.6 vs exact (2k rows), parallel==serial, file-backed reopen (tests/integration.rs). 287 lib + 6 integration green.
- Bench: nidus_regression.rs gains a parallel_search_binary sweep group (build_store_threaded now takes Option<Quantization>).
- Docs: SPEC §9 binary moved deferred->shipped; CLAUDE.md deferred list + search.rs comment; config.rs quantization doc.
- Gates: just ci GREEN (fmt+clippy -D+test), cargo check --features cli GREEN, just deps still empty (no new crates), Miri-clean on kernel + pure binary store tests.

REMAINING: the shared scan-order cache (folded-in nidus-dxt) is NOT yet done — that's the next increment. Binary quant is independently complete/shippable.

title: Add range + negation filter predicates (Lt/Le/Gt/Ge, Ne/NotIn)
description: The metadata filter surface is Eq/Glob/In, AND-only — too thin for real semantic-search consumers, who routinely need numeric/date ranges (created_at > X, version >= N) and exclusions (status != archived, tag not in {...}). Add range comparisons (Lt/Le/Gt/Ge) and negation (Ne/NotIn) as new Predicate variants. Pure-logic, Miri-clean, zero deps, no FFI. Predicate derives serde and CLI(--where JSON)/server take Filter via serde, so new variants flow through lib + CLI + HTTP for free; just matching logic + Value ordering + tests + docs. Additive and backward-compatible (old filters unchanged). OR/disjunction is a separate, bigger follow-up.
design: New Predicate variants: Ne(key,Value), NotIn(key,Vec<Value>), Lt/Le/Gt/Ge(key,Value). Semantics consistent with existing predicates — a predicate is a positive assertion about a PRESENT attribute: absent key or incomparable type => no match (same as Eq/Glob/In returning false on absent). Range ordering is same-type only via a value_cmp(&Value,&Value)->Option<Ordering> helper: Int/Int numeric, Str/Str lexical, Bool/Bool (false<true); Null/List/cross-type => None => no match. Ne/NotIn match iff key present AND not-equal/not-in. Document the absent-key rule explicitly in SPEC §7.1. Alternative considered (absent satisfies Ne, SQL-ish) rejected for consistency; an explicit Exists predicate or OR support can cover it later.
acceptance_criteria: Lt/Le/Gt/Ge/Ne/NotIn match correctly for Int/Str/Bool; absent key and incomparable type => no match; cross-type => no match; List/Null never range-match. Round-trips through serde (CLI --where JSON + server Filter). Inline Miri-clean tests cover present/absent/wrong-type/boundary. SPEC §7.1 + search guide + CLI --where help updated. just ci + just ci-cli + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented: Ne/NotIn + Lt/Le/Gt/Ge predicates with same-type orderable comparison (value_cmp) and a consistent present-key rule. Pure-logic/Miri-clean, flows through CLI+server via serde. PR #12.

title: Improve parallel-scan scaling (parallelize quant path; cut serial overhead)
description:
Opt-in parallel scan (Config::query_threads) currently gives only ~1.2-1.6x at 4-8 threads. The f32 path is memory-bandwidth-bound (streams the whole matrix), so it's near its physical ceiling there. The real levers:

1. Parallelize the QUANTIZED first-pass. It's the biggest opportunity: int8 moves 4x fewer bytes, so it's compute- not bandwidth-bound and should scale with threads far better than f32. search_quantized() is currently single-threaded regardless of query_threads -- wire the same std::thread::scope chunk+merge pattern (already used for the f32 path in score_chunk/search) into the int8 first pass, then keep the f32 rerank serial (it's only top_k*rescore rows).

2. Cut serial overhead (Amdahl). The scan buffer build + sort_unstable_by_key runs single-threaded before the parallel region and caps speedup. Options: build per-thread scan buffers, or skip the global row-sort on the parallel path (the per-chunk heaps don't need a globally sorted scan -- the storage-order prefetch benefit is per-chunk).

3. Make PARALLEL_SCAN_FLOOR dim-aware. Per-row cost scales with dim, so a fixed 4096-row floor is too coarse; threshold on rows*dim (work) instead of rows.

Validate each with 'just bench-crit parallel_search' (extend it to also sweep the quantized path). Keep it pure-safe-Rust, no new deps, opt-in. Set honest expectations: f32 won't scale linearly; the quant path is where threads should pay off.
design: Reuse score_chunk + the thread::scope merge already in store::search for the int8 path. Don't add a persistent thread pool (spawn cost is microseconds vs millisecond scans -- not the bottleneck).
close_reason: Parallelized the int8 first-pass (~2.4x at 4 threads vs f32's ~1.4x plateau), skipped the global row-sort on the parallel path (per-chunk sort instead), and made the scan floor dim-aware (rows*dim work). bench-crit parallel_search extended to sweep the quant path.

title: Proactive max_vector_bytes cap + Nidus::footprint() introspection
description:
The only exhaustion defense reliable under cgroup/overcommit (where the kernel SIGKILLs before malloc returns null, so try_reserve never fires) is to refuse work before allocating.

- src/config.rs: add pub max_vector_bytes: Option<u64> (default None, no behavior change) + builder setter. Caps the vector matrix (rows*dim*4) — the predictable host-facing number. Bounds physical rows incl. not-yet-compacted dead rows, so compact() reclaims headroom (document).
- Enforcement: store::upsert projects (row_count+records.len())*dim*4; if > cap, bail! with a capacity message BEFORE any append (clean refusal, no rollback, store stays usable for reads/search). DataSegment::open (cap passed through from Store::open): if on-disk data_bytes already exceeds cap, bail! BEFORE the big allocation — turns 'abort on open' into a clear error.
- Introspection: src/model.rs add pub struct Footprint { rows, dead_rows, dimension, vector_bytes, doc_count } (cheap, no alloc). Store::footprint(); expose Nidus::footprint() and re-export Footprint from crate root in src/lib.rs.

Tests (in-memory + file): max_vector_bytes rejects over-budget upsert with store still searchable; open with cap below file size returns clean Err (not panic) mentioning capacity; footprint() field math before/after upsert/delete/compact.
acceptance_criteria: Config::max_vector_bytes enforced on upsert (refuse before append) and open (refuse before allocation); Nidus::footprint() returns rows/dead_rows/dimension/vector_bytes/doc_count; default None preserves current behavior; just ci + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented + verified: atomic appends & all-or-nothing upsert (data+log rollback), try_reserve OOM→Err across vector matrix + index maps with streaming open, max_vector_bytes cap + footprint(), and per-module SPEC.md consolidated into root SPEC.md. just ci + just miri green; cargo doc clean.

title: try_reserve: turn OOM aborts into Result, and halve the open() peak
description:
Vec/HashMap growth aborts the process (handle_alloc_error) on allocator-null OOM instead of returning Err. Convert to anyhow::Err at every site std allows.

- Vec sites (try_reserve/try_reserve_exact, map failure to anyhow!): DataSegment::append (before extend_from_slice), compact's new_rows, rewrite's to_vec, store::search scan, upsert new_rows, get_all result.
- DataSegment::open: replace read-all-bytes-then-decode (raw Vec<u8> + decoded Vec<f32>, ~2x peak) with try_reserve_exact the final Vec<f32> once, then stream the file in bounded byte chunks converting LE->f32 into it. Single allocation, fallible, peak ≈ steady state.
- HashMap sites: try_reserve on collections and col.docs before batch insert in upsert and in the open-replay loop.
- OpLog::open: pre-try_reserve before read_to_end using file length.

std limitation (document inline, NOT a follow-up): BTreeMap and String have no try_reserve, so attrs (BTreeMap<String,Value>) clones and id Strings in upsert/replay stay abort-on-OOM — a language constraint. Small relative to the N*dim*4 matrix; the Gap-3 cap covers dominant memory. Add // NOTE: at each such site + a durability-docs line.

Tests (pure, Miri-clean): float-reserve helper with absurd n returns Err without allocating.
acceptance_criteria: Allocator-null OOM on the vector matrix + index HashMaps surfaces as anyhow::Err, not abort; DataSegment::open uses a single Vec<f32> allocation (no 2x transient); BTreeMap/String std limitation documented inline + in docs; just ci + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented + verified: atomic appends & all-or-nothing upsert (data+log rollback), try_reserve OOM→Err across vector matrix + index maps with streaming open, max_vector_bytes cap + footprint(), and per-module SPEC.md consolidated into root SPEC.md. just ci + just miri green; cargo doc clean.

title: nidus dot() is a scalar reduction — ~1.6x slower than LanceDB SIMD at scale
description: Finding from nidus-bench (epic nidus-rq9): on exact brute-force cosine KNN, nidus is a reproducible ~1.6x slower than LanceDB at 50k-100k x 384 (nidus ~22ms vs lancedb ~13.5ms p50 @100k; stable across runs, all recall 100%). Diagnosis: NOT variance, NOT parallelism (capping RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1/TOKIO_WORKER_THREADS=1 left lancedb unchanged). Root cause: src/search/mod.rs dot() is a sequential f32 reduction (a.iter().zip(b).map(|(x,y)|x*y).sum()) which LLVM can't auto-vectorize without fast-math (FP non-associativity), so it runs scalar. LanceDB's lance-linalg uses explicit SIMD (NEON). nidus sits mid-pack: faster than DuckDB (~30ms), ~1.6x slower than LanceDB. Proposed fix (stays pure safe stable Rust, no unsafe/fast-math/deps): rewrite dot as a chunked reduction with N independent accumulators (e.g. 8 lanes) so each lane is an independent sum LLVM CAN vectorize, combined at the end. Caveat: changes summation order -> slightly different f32 rounding (may reorder exact-tie boundaries; benchmark recall tolerant). Verify with just bench all + just bench-crit before/after.
notes:
FIXED — outcome far exceeded the 1.6x parity goal: nidus is now the FASTEST engine in every benchmark cell (2-6x faster than LanceDB, 4-12x faster than DuckDB), recall 100%.

Three stacked fixes (all pure safe stable Rust, no unsafe/fast-math/deps):
1. SIMD-friendly dot (src/search/mod.rs): 8 independent accumulator lanes (chunks_exact(8) + pairwise fold + scalar tail) so each lane is its own associative reduction LLVM can auto-vectorize. Closed most of the kernel gap (dim=768 reached parity).
2. No-alloc scan (src/store/mod.rs search()): TopK now carries borrowed (&str,&str) during the scan instead of eagerly building (col.to_string(), id.clone()) per row — eliminated ~50M throwaway String allocs at 100k×384. Strings/attrs materialized only for the <=k survivors.
3. Row-sorted sequential scan (src/store/mod.rs search()): collect in-scope filter-passing rows as cheap borrowed tuples, sort_unstable_by_key(row), then walk the data matrix in storage order. The 159MB matrix dwarfs L3, so the old HashMap-iteration order was DRAM-miss-bound at every row boundary; sequential order lets the prefetcher stream it. THIS was the dominant win — criterion search/100k×768 -69.8%.

Numbers (p50, 100k vectors): 384/k10 nidus 5.44ms vs lance 12.29 vs duck 32.29; 768/k10 nidus 8.09 vs lance 24.78 vs duck 69.54; 768/k100 nidus 8.57 vs lance 53.16 vs duck 64.99.

Future (separate issue, NOT done here): the row-sorted scan allocates+sorts an n-element Vec per query; doc set is static between writes, so the sorted order could be cached and invalidated on upsert/delete. Deferred to keep this change focused.
close_reason: Exceeded the goal: nidus is now the fastest engine in every bench cell (2-6x over LanceDB), recall 100%, via SIMD-friendly dot + alloc-free + storage-order scan. Follow-up nidus-dxt filed.

title: cluster mode follow-ups: CAS-fenced manifest writes, real-bucket verification
description: Phase 5 (nidus-vuq.5) shipped cooperating-instances cluster mode: Config::cluster, a renewing+fencing writer lease (op-driven, no background thread), and the manifest as a universal commit counter so ReadOnly readers refresh() per commit. Hardening follow-ups, none blocking: (1) CAS-fenced manifest/segment writes — the lease is renewed+verified at each batch START (the primary fence), but a writer that pauses > lock_ttl *mid-batch* (e.g. a long GC pause between renew and the manifest PUT) could still be superseded yet clobber. Close this with a conditional/compare-and-swap manifest PUT (S3 If-Match:<etag> / GCS ifGenerationMatch=<gen>) keyed on the expected current version — needs ETag/generation plumbing beyond the existing create-if-absent (nidus-a7c). (2) Real S3/GCS cluster verification — the lease takes the race-free try_create_exclusive path; verify acquire/renew/fence/takeover against real buckets (the in-RAM InMemObjectStore covers the logic). (3) Tier-aware refresh in cluster — adopt the shared memory-tier working-set snapshot on refresh instead of replaying the log (overlaps nidus-bdg). (4) Doc/guard the single idle-writer-past-TTL takeover window (lock_ttl tuning).
notes: SHIPPED (PR: cluster-cas-and-incremental-refresh, v0.23.0): CAS-fenced cluster writes. New Persistence::get_cas/put_cas (opaque token; CasOutcome) implemented for S3 (If-Match/If-None-Match ETag), GCS (ifGenerationMatch), and the in-RAM test store; try_create_exclusive now delegates to put_cas (deduped). Every cluster-writer durable object write (data/log/segments via ObjectAppender, manifest via persist_manifest) is now a compare-and-swap on the version last seen, so a writer superseded mid-batch is REFUSED rather than clobbering committed data — closes the mid-batch split-brain window the per-batch lease could not. Verified in-RAM (cluster_cas_fences_a_superseded_writer_and_preserves_committed_data, cluster_manifest_commit_is_compare_and_swapped) + offline S3 mock round-trip + GCS URI unit tests; all Miri-clean. REMAINING (keep open): real S3/GCS bucket verification of acquire/renew/fence/takeover + CAS (cannot run cloud in dev env); optional lock_ttl tuning docs. Tier-aware refresh sub-item delivered under nidus-bdg.

title: refresh follow-ups: incremental delta refresh, tier-aware adopt, change-callback
description: Phase 4 shipped Nidus::refresh() (PR for nidus-vuq.4): a ReadOnly reader re-reads the manifest+log and adopts a writer's newer state in place. It currently does a FULL re-open of the segment set + a FULL log replay on any change. Follow-ups to make it cheaper / richer: (1) Incremental refresh — re-read only the active segment's appended-rows delta and the new log tail since loaded_log_offset, instead of re-opening every segment and replaying from zero (O(delta) not O(N)); seal/compaction can stay on the full path. (2) Tier-aware refresh — when a shared memory tier is configured, try_adopt its working-set snapshot (matching row_count+watermark) on refresh instead of replaying the log, mirroring the open path. (3) Optional change-notification hook so a host can learn a refresh found new state without polling. All additive; none needed until a real cooperating-reader workload asks for it (single-node refresh is already correct and cheap-when-unchanged).
notes: SHIPPED (PR: cluster-cas-and-incremental-refresh, v0.23.0): incremental + tier-aware refresh. refresh() now (1) adopts a shared memory-tier snapshot matching the new row-count+watermark, skipping the log replay (mirrors open); (2) on the no-restructure path (segment LIST unchanged — plain appends, no seal/compaction) re-reads ONLY the active segment object and reuses every immutable segment via Segments::reopen_active/install_active, avoiding the dominant object-store cost of re-fetching the whole set; a seal/compaction (list changed) takes the full re-open. 'restructured' keys on the segment list, not the manifest version (which in cluster mode is the per-commit counter). Atomic: all fallible IO into locals, one infallible swap. Verified: cluster_incremental_refresh_skips_immutable_segments (GET-count asserts the immutable base is not re-fetched) + existing refresh_* parity tests; Miri-clean. REMAINING (keep open, low priority): true O(delta) active-segment read via a range/partial GET (currently re-reads the whole active object) needs a Persistence range-read primitive; optional change-notification hook (refresh already returns adopted? bool, so not needed until a real workload asks).

title: mmap follow-ups: runtime seal-to-mmap, ReadOnly active-segment map, object-store cold cache, madvise, big-endian
description: Phase 3 (nidus-vuq.3) shipped per-segment mmap as an open-time decision over local-FS immutable segments. Deferred, additive follow-ups over the same seam: (1) runtime seal-to-mmap — a long-running writer keeps just-sealed segments in RAM until reopen; dropping the RAM copy and mapping on seal would give >RAM within one process lifetime. (2) mmap the active/last segment in ReadOnly opens — a search-only reader of a single large segment can't currently map it (only non-active segments are mapped). (3) object-store cold cache — download remote (s3/gs) segments to a local cache file then mmap; mmap is local-FS-only today. (4) madvise(SEQUENTIAL/RANDOM/WILLNEED) hints to tune paging for the scan pattern. (5) big-endian hosts fall back to RAM load (the zero-copy cast is little-endian only) — add a converting mmap path if a BE target ever matters. None are needed at nidus's local-first scale; file-and-defer.

title: Per-segment IVF: persist caches + parallel/quantized walks + background merge
description:
Follow-ups deferred from Phase 2 (nidus-vuq.2), all additive over the shipped segment+IVF format:
1. Persist per-segment IVF indexes as their own cache objects (e.g. seg-NNNNNNNN.ivf) so open() loads instead of rebuilding from scratch. Today every per-segment IVF is rebuilt on open (immutable segments, cheap k-means) — fine for now, but a cache avoids O(indexed rows) k-means on every open of a large store. Mirror the existing ann/fts cache codec + validity-key pattern (dedupe).
2. Parallel + quantized per-segment walks: build/search the per-segment IVFs under query_threads and let them score quant codes (the global ann path already does both via Walk). Today per-segment uses Walk::exact, serial-ish build.
3. Expose per-segment IVF tuning (n_lists/n_probe/overscan/seed) instead of the fixed AnnConfig::ivf() defaults.
4. Background segment-merge / compaction of many small sealed segments into fewer larger indexed ones (SPEC §14.2 'background merge').

title: Phase 5: cluster mode (shared backend + writer lease + fencing)
description: Cluster mode as a CONSEQUENCE of the segment model: segments+WAL+manifest on a SHARED persistence backend are the shared truth; instances are stateless caches. One writer appends segments + advances the manifest, elected by the §6.3 writer lock (race-free over object stores, nidus-a7c) evolved into a heartbeated LEASE; fence segment/manifest writes so a superseded-but-alive writer can't clobber (reuse the a7c conditional-PUT plumbing). Readers refresh per phase 4. Requires shared persistence AND shared memory tier; reject local FS/local RAM for cluster mode with a clear error. SPEC §14.6 phase 5.
close_reason: Shipped (0.22.0): cooperating-instances cluster mode via Config::cluster — the SPEC §14.6 phase-5 shared-backend model (1 writer + N readers), lightweight (no coordinator/replication/rebalancing). (1) Writer LEASE (src/backend/object.rs ClusterLease): the object writer-lock evolved to carry an owner token, acquired race-free via try_create_exclusive, RENEWED op-driven on every write batch in check_writable() (no background thread, honouring the sync/no-threads design), and FENCING — a superseded writer (taken over while paused past lock_ttl) fails its next write instead of clobbering. (2) Manifest = universal COMMIT COUNTER: every durable batch bumps+republishes the manifest version (Segments::bump_version + note_cluster_commit in maybe_sync/upsert/flush), so a ReadOnly reader detects any commit (not just seals) with one manifest read via refresh() (phase 4) — works on object stores where log length isn't live cross-process. (3) Config gating: cluster requires shared object-store persistence AND a shared memory tier; local FS / process RAM rejected with a clear error. No new deps; §4 public API unchanged (cluster is config-only). Tests: 7 store-level (object_backed, Miri-clean) + 3 lease unit (backend, Miri-clean) + 1 file-backed local-FS rejection. Follow-ups (CAS-fenced manifest writes for the mid-batch-pause window, real-bucket verification, tier-aware refresh): nidus-ahw. Epic nidus-vuq now fully complete (5/5).

title: Phase 4: manifest-versioned reader refresh
description: Version the manifest; a reader detects a newer manifest and adopts it atomically (swap the pointer, then drop segments no longer referenced) — a consistent snapshot, never a torn mix. Foundation for cooperating instances seeing fresh writes without a full reopen. SPEC §14.6 phase 4 + consistency note.
close_reason: Shipped (0.21.0): Nidus::refresh() -> Result<bool> advances a lock-free ReadOnly reader to a writer's newer committed state in place — re-reads the manifest, and when the version advanced (seal/compaction) or the log grew (append/delete) re-opens the segment set + replays the log into a fresh index at one consistent point, swapped in atomically (segment-open/log-read run before any self mutation, so a concurrent-compaction race leaves the prior snapshot serving). Cheap no-op when current (manifest read + log stat). Composes with mmap, per-segment IVF, quantization. Change detection: manifest.version (Segments::version()) + loaded_log_offset watermark. New public API additive (SPEC §14.5 honoured). Follow-ups in nidus-bdg.

title: Phase 3: mmap per segment (larger-than-RAM on one node)
description: Apply the §9 mmap seam scoped to a segment: hold/mmap a subset of segments, leave cold segments on the backend until touched. The one conscious FFI opt-in (relaxes zero-FFI for this path only); keep it off the default. SPEC §14.6 phase 3.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #35 (commit 3eeaaa9, 0.20.0): per-segment mmap via Config::mmap, default-on dep with one scoped #[allow(unsafe_code)] on the lone Mmap::map call; crate root forbid->deny(unsafe_code). Local-FS + sealed + little-endian only; identical results to all-RAM path.

title: Phase 3: S3 + GCS live backends (sans-IO clients, whole-object/segmented writes)
description: S3 (rusty-s3, sans-IO, Sigv4/HMAC) and GCS (tame-gcs, sans-IO, OAuth2 svc-account) live backends behind s3:// and gs://. They are DIFFERENT APIs needing different clients — not one cloud backend. No append on objects: ship only the small data/log delta and drop+rebuild ann/fts on open (cheap writes, costlier open), or add segmented objects for incremental writes later. Whole-object PUT is atomic so caches get atomicity free. Cross-process exclusion via conditional-PUT (weaker than O_EXCL). Gated behind a non-default feature; the TLS dep follows the DECISION in nidus-870.1.
acceptance_criteria: s3:// and gs:// stores open/upsert/search/reopen against real (or MinIO/emulator) buckets; caches rebuilt on open; conditional-PUT lock; feature-gated per the §13.6 decision; version bumped. Honors whichever crypto path nidus-870.1 selects.
notes: S3 SHIPPED (src/backend/s3.rs): whole-object get/put/delete/list over sans-IO rusty-s3 + ureq (rustls+ring), s3://<bucket>[/<prefix>], AWS env creds/region/endpoint. Object backend (appender=None) → snapshot/whole-object use, not live-append-backing. Verified: offline presigned-URL/prefix tests + a localhost mock S3 round-trip (put/get/list/delete/404). Deps in DEFAULT build, clean whole-crate build 7s. GCS STILL PENDING (tame-gcs + tame-oauth; harder to verify — no easy mock for the oauth token dance). README/SPEC/CLAUDE/docs positioning updated (build-speed-not-zero-C; ring allowed).
close_reason: Both S3 (a9..) and GCS (this) persistence backends shipped as whole-object Persistence impls (snapshot/whole-object use; appender=None, no live-append-backing). Deps in default build; build 7s; positioning updated. Live-on-cloud append-backing was never in scope (it's the heavy O(store) mode).

title: Phase 2: Shared memory tier (Redis/Valkey/Memcached) — model (a), loaded into local RAM
description: First network backend and the proof the trait holds for a LIVE store. Use redis-rs in blocking mode (pure Rust, sync). redis:// needs no TLS. Maps the append capability natively (APPEND / streams), so it exercises the live write path without the object-store whole-object-rewrite problem. Gated behind a non-default feature so the pure library is untouched. Document the open-cost / shared-state-across-processes tradeoffs (SPEC §13.4/§13.5).
acceptance_criteria: redis:// store opens, upserts, searches, and reopens against a real Redis/Valkey; append maps to a native incremental op; feature-gated; version bumped.
notes: RECATEGORIZED: NOT a durable storage backend with native append. It is the MEMORY TIER (SPEC §13.3) — a shared, rebuildable cache of the serialized in-RAM working set (vectors + derived indexes), pulled into local RAM to serve. Wins: N stateless workers share one copy; restart = load not rebuild. Pure-Rust sync (redis-rs blocking, plain TCP redis://, no TLS). Memcached = evictable cache-only, weakest. Rebuildable from persistence, so empty/evicted is never fatal.
close_reason: Implemented: Redis/Valkey/KeyDB/Dragonfly memory tier (RedisTier) wired e2e (publish-on-flush, adopt-on-open); live S3/GCS backing via ObjectAppender + advisory lock; CLI --persistence/--memory; backup/restore over any backend. Tests (incl. Miri-clean in-RAM e2e + RESP mock) green.

title: Docs recipe: running nidus across a few boxes (no built cluster machinery)

Write a docs recipe: run one nidus instance per box, do query fan-out + top-k merge on the client side (sound because all instances share one embedding space). Explicitly document what is intentionally out of scope (coordinator/replication/fault-tolerance). Docs only — no code in core.

title: Cache the row-sorted scan order across queries
description: search() now collects in-scope rows into a Vec and sort_unstable_by_key(row) on EVERY query to get sequential data-matrix access (the big win in nidus-33k). The doc set only changes on upsert/delete, so this sorted order is recomputed redundantly across the many queries between writes. Optimization: maintain a row-sorted index (or lazily cache + invalidate on write) so steady-state search skips the per-query O(n) alloc + O(n log n) sort. Must stay correct under multi-collection scope, filters, dead rows (append-only overwrite), and Miri-clean. Measure with just bench-crit against the nidus-33k-final baseline; only worth it if it beats the already-strong current numbers without complicating the write path. Filed from nidus-33k.
notes:
Measured 2026-06-05 while doing nidus-8gx (which changed the sort landscape).

FINDINGS:
- sort_unstable_by_key on the scan is ~2.16 ms standalone at n=100k, dim=768
  (micro-bench: clone+sort and sort-only both ~2.15 ms). That's ~29% of the
  7.45 ms serial f32 query, and it scales with n. So caching it is a real win
  for LARGE-n SINGLE-query SERIAL search.
- The sort itself is ESSENTIAL and stays: skipping it (random HashMap-order
  access) blows the serial query from 7.45 ms to 22 ms (~3x slower) because
  sequential data-matrix access is the cache-friendliness win from nidus-33k.
  dxt must KEEP sorted access and only remove the per-query RECOMPUTE.

POST-8gx SCOPE NARROWED:
- 8gx made the parallel path sort PER-CHUNK inside workers (global sort skipped
  there). So dxt now only helps the SERIAL path directly. A cached global order
  could let the parallel path skip per-chunk sorts too (composes well), but the
  at-scale recommendation is now query_threads / quant, not serial.

WRITE-PATH / SYNC TENSION (the issue's gating concern):
- Store is used as Arc<RwLock<Nidus>>, so the cache cannot use RefCell (not
  Sync). Clean options: (a) lazy global cache behind RwLock<Option<Vec>> +
  dirty flag (write path just flips the flag; ~2% extra RAM for duplicated
  id/col strings; one rebuild after each write batch); (b) per-collection
  append-order index exploiting monotonic rows (no RwLock, but overwrites/
  deletes need validation-or-fallback + multi-collection needs k-way merge);
  (c) row-major doc table refactor (eliminates the sort for ALL paths, cleanest
  end state, but large higher-risk refactor across every read/write path).

DECISION 2026-06-05: deferred by user. Benefit is real but narrow post-8gx and
every clean capture touches RAM or the write path. Revisit if a consumer hits
large-n serial search as the hot path. If picked up, (a) is the recommended
low-risk start; (c) is the right long-term shape on its own branch.
2026-06-09: Pulled into nidus-3zg (binary quant + shared scan-order cache). The scan-order cache is specced as a component there — implement together so all scan paths (serial/parallel f32, int8, binary) share one row-sorted index. Tracking the cache work under 3zg; keeping dxt open as the linked detail/rationale.
